critic_raw: The evaluation appears to cherry-pick results by only reporting performance above the 0.92 confidence threshold. Excluding all abstained tickets inflates the reported precision and harmful-error rate because those tickets are, by definition, the ones the model is most uncertain about. If the purpose is to evaluate whether the system is safe and useful, the evaluation should account for the full ticket population including the 63% that goes to humans. There is also an unresolved question about whether the threshold was truly prospective or whether the 0.92 value emerged from iterative tuning on development data to hit the 1% error target.
defender_raw: The pre-registration answers the cherry-picking concern directly. The 0.92 threshold and the 1% harmful-error ceiling and 30% coverage floor were all defined before model training began. The evaluation was structured this way by design because the product is a selective-prediction pipeline, not a universal classifier. The claim being made is about the selective system, and the correct metric for that system is performance on accepted tickets plus coverage. The 63% of tickets routed to humans is not an omission — it is reported as the abstention rate and is part of the system design. A model that routes uncertain cases to humans is operating exactly as intended. The threshold was not selected based on the test set.

debate_rounds.critic: If the pre-registration documentation is genuine — defining the 0.92 threshold, 1% error ceiling, and 30% coverage floor before training — then the selective reporting concern is resolved. The remaining question is whether the evaluation captures the full fairness picture: are abstentions uniformly distributed across ticket categories, or does the model systematically decline to answer certain types of tickets? A selective-prediction system that achieves good aggregate performance but disproportionately abstains on one customer segment may still be problematic even if the top-line metrics are sound.
debate_rounds.defender: That is a valid fairness consideration but it is distinct from the question of whether the evaluation methodology is sound. The prompt states the report provides an abstention rate and that coverage is reported at the system level. A category-level breakdown would be useful for a fairness audit but its absence does not invalidate the pre-specified evaluation. The appropriate framing is: the core evaluation is sound; a supplementary fairness analysis by ticket category would be the recommended next step.

adjudication_raw: The critic's initial concern about cherry-picking is fully resolved by the pre-registration, as both parties agree after round 1. The residual fairness concern about category-level abstention distribution is a legitimate extension of the analysis but does not constitute a methodological flaw in the evaluation described. Defense wins. The evaluation protocol is appropriate for a pre-specified selective-prediction system.
all_issues_raised: selective reporting concern — resolved by pre-registration
category-level abstention distribution as potential fairness concern

empirical_test.condition: Verify pre-registration documentation shows 0.92 threshold, 1% error ceiling, and 30% coverage floor were established before test evaluation; confirm test set metrics meet both targets
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Threshold was adjusted after reviewing test outcomes, or test set fails either the harmful-error or coverage target
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Pre-registration documentation confirms prospective threshold and targets; untouched test set meets both harmful-error and coverage requirements
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Coverage meets target but one ticket category shows substantially higher abstention rates that are not disclosed
